# Transcrição: 02-Developing Your Central Message

- **Fonte:** 02-Developing Your Central Message
- **Duração:** 08:59
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Crafting clear and concise messages is critical to your success as a communicator. Messages that are tightly structured help your audience to remember them and make it easier for them to act on what you're saying. A logical structure will help us to accomplish our goal. Many of us, when we communicate, simply itemize information. We list out what we want to say. On slides we use bullet points. Our brains are not wired for lists of information. In fact, think about the last time you went to the grocery store. How many items did you have to itemize before you had to write your list? If you're like me, it was only three or four. We need to leverage the way our brain is set up for information. We like information that's logically connected, a beginning, a middle, and an end. By providing structure for your audience, you help set their expectations so they know where you're going. You connect ideas together so they don't get lost, and ultimately you make it simple for them to remember. A structure is nothing more than a logical connection of ideas. We're all familiar with one popular structure used in most advertisements on television. Problem, solution, benefit. The commercial starts with some issue, challenge, or problem. They then offer up their product or their service as a way of solving it, and then they share with you the benefits. By structuring the information this way, it makes it easy for you to remember what's involved. So let's dive deeper into a structure. The first thing we have to remember is our communication needs to be goal-driven. What is it that we want our audience to know, feel, and do? In the prior session, I talked about the importance of a goal. Once we have our goal, it helps us figure out what we put into our structure and what we leave out. Once we have the idea of what we want to achieve, we have to think what are the best structures I can use to get my information across. So beyond problem, solution, benefit, let me share some other useful structures. For example, you might use comparison, contrast, conclusion. You might compare two ideas or products or services, then share how they're different, that's their contrast, and then your conclusion that you offer. You might be explaining how something is evolving over time. You could use the chronological structure, past, present, future. My favorite structure, and one I encourage all of you to consider, is three simple questions. What? So what? Now what? What is your idea, your service, your product? So what is, why is it important and relevant to the audience you're talking to? And then finally, the now what is what you'd like to do next?

## [03:02 - 06:05]

Maybe it's showing them a demonstration or taking their questions or setting up another meeting. By picking one of these structures, you help yourself prioritize what to say and how to say it, and you make it easier for your audience. In essence, it's a recipe. It's a recipe that allows you to easily and quickly formulate your thoughts. It also helps you link ideas together. Many people get lost in their communication if it's not structured well. Think of yourself as a tour guide when you communicate. Every good tour guide sets expectations up front. They tell you where you're going and where you're not going. Structure does that. And any good tour guide will tell you the place you lose people the most is when you move from one area to the next. A good transition connecting where you've been to where you're going helps keep people together. Having a structure and thinking through different structures allows you to focus group, if you will, the most effective way to communicate your information. Not only does it help you prioritize what to say, it helps you remember how to say it. We want to avoid memorizing word for word what we say because when we memorize it actually challenges us to focus on what we memorized and the actual communication. If you have a structure, that recipe, it actually provides you with a roadmap. It tells you where to start and where to end. So by doing this, we can actually make sure that we stay on track and deliver the information that we need. Having a structure is core to communicating effectively. By having a structure and making sure it's relevant and salient to your audience, you will be more focused in what you're saying. You will be more clear and concise. ## [06:05 - 08:59]

How you start matters. Most people start by introducing themselves. Hi, my name is. That's boring and often disengaging. And silly too, because often you're showing something that says your name in the title of your presentation. I believe all pitches and presentations should start like action movies. With action, ask a question, take a poll, tell a story, reveal a startling statistic. From doing that, you draw your audience in. And then you can introduce yourself. And then you can share what your topic is. So let's start in an engaging way. And as part of that start, we preview where we're taking our audience. We share with them our structure. So I might start by saying, how many of you have ever struggled with this? I take the poll and upon the response, I then say, well, today we're going to focus on ways to help you manage that. We're going to dig deeper into the actual issue. I'll suggest ways to address it. And ultimately, we'll discuss how you benefit. So by getting the attention of the audience and then sharing where I'm taking them, I am likely to keep them with me. Now we also have to think about how we end as well. Many of us are just so relieved to get to the end of our presentation or pitches, we just stop talking. We remember what we hear first and last the most. So it makes sense to actually invest effort into how you conclude. Now we have to realize as soon as I signal I'm ending, people start paying attention to other things.
